segments.title: Force K as a convoy-interdiction force
segments.summary: Force K is presented as a surface force built to sever Axis resupply convoys to North Africa, beyond Malta's gradual submarine and air attrition.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke contrasts the slow cumulative pressure of submarines and air strikes from Malta with the effect of a surface group that can destroy a convoy outright. The 1941-42 Force Ks therefore serve sea-denial: their task is to make Axis resupply of North Africa too dangerous to sustain confidently.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts Malta-based attrition with a surface force stopping an entire Axis convoy pipeline.

segments.title: Force K's 1941 raiding group
segments.summary: The 1941 Force K combines the cruisers Aurora and Penelope with L-class destroyers and substantial anti-aircraft armament for convoy raids.
segments.body: The group pairs two light cruisers with destroyers, giving it the speed and striking power needed to hunt shipping. Dr. Clarke also highlights its anti-aircraft armament, an important protection for a Mediterranean raiding force operating under air threat.

evidence.note: The transcript names Aurora, Penelope, L-class destroyers, anti-aircraft armament, and raiding in 1941.

segments.title: Force K's 1942 cruiser-destroyer balance
segments.summary: The 1942 formation of four destroyers and three Dido-class cruisers could overwhelm most convoy escorts, but not a convoy protected by battleships.
segments.body: The later Force K is described as four destroyers from the 14th Flotilla alongside three Dido-class light cruisers. That combination could stop most forces likely to escort a convoy, while the prospect of Italian battleship cover marks the limit of its advantage.

evidence.note: The transcript describes four 14th Flotilla destroyers, three Dido-class light cruisers, and the exception of Italian battleship escort cover.
